title: Quiz: Classical Algorithms
description: Test your understanding of fundamental classical algorithms

questions.question: What is the time complexity of binary search on a sorted array of n elements?
questions.explanation: Binary search halves the search space each step, requiring log₂(n) comparisons in the worst case.

questions.question: Which sorting algorithm has the best average-case time complexity?
questions.explanation: Merge Sort achieves O(n log n) in all cases, which is optimal for comparison-based sorting.

questions.question: Linear search requires the input array to be sorted.
questions.explanation: False! Linear search works on any array, sorted or unsorted. Binary search requires sorted arrays.

questions.question: Dijkstra's algorithm finds:

options.text: The longest path in a graph
The shortest path from a source to all other nodes

questions.explanation: Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest path from a single source node to all other nodes in a weighted graph with non-negative edges.

questions.question: What traversal order does BFS (Breadth-First Search) use?
questions.explanation: BFS explores all neighbors at the current depth before moving to nodes at the next depth level, using a queue data structure.

questions.question: Which algorithm can Grover's quantum algorithm potentially speed up?
questions.explanation: Grover's algorithm provides quadratic speedup for unstructured search (O(√N) vs O(N)). Binary search is already O(log N) and doesn't benefit.

questions.question: Quick Sort has O(n log n) time complexity in all cases.
questions.explanation: False! Quick Sort's worst case is O(n²) when the pivot selection is poor (e.g., already sorted array with first element as pivot). Average case is O(n log n).

questions.question: What data structure does DFS (Depth-First Search) typically use?
questions.explanation: DFS uses a stack (explicitly or via recursion) to explore as deep as possible before backtracking. BFS uses a queue.
